bibliographic_data.title: The Complexity of Online Bribery in Sequential Elections (Extended Abstract)
bibliographic_data.abstract: Prior work on the complexity of bribery assumes that the bribery happens simultaneously, and that the briber has full knowledge of all voters’ votes. But neither of those assumptions always holds. In many real-world settings, votes come in sequentially, and the briber may have a use-it-or-lose-it moment to decide whether to bribe/alter a given vote, and at the time of making that decision, the briber may not know what votes remaining voters are planning on casting. In this paper, we introduce a model for, and initiate the study of, bribery in such an online, sequential setting. We show that even for election systems whose winner-determination problem is polynomial-time computable, an online, sequential setting may vastly increase the complexity of bribery, in fact jumping the problem up to completeness for high levels of the polynomial hierarchy or even PSPACE. On the other hand, we show that for some natural, important election systems, such a dramatic complexity increase does not occur, and we pinpoint the complexity of their bribery problems in the online, sequential setting.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper introduces a model for online, sequential bribery in elections, where the briber can only decide to bribe a voter at the moment they vote, and may not know future votes. This model significantly increases the complexity of bribery problems.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use alternating Turing machines to prove upper bounds on the complexity of online bribery problems. They also provide examples showing that for certain election systems, these problems can be PSPACE-complete or Πp2k+1-complete.
outcomes: Established upper bounds (PSPACE and Πp2k+1) for online bribery in general cases and with a restriction on the number of bribes.
Demonstrated that there are election systems where these upper bounds are tight, i.e., PSPACE-completeness or Πp2k+1-completeness holds.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, we introduce and study a model for online bribery in sequential elections. Unlike previous work which assumes simultaneous voting and full knowledge of all votes, our model considers the scenario where voters vote sequentially and the briber can only decide to bribe at the moment a voter is about to cast their vote. This setting significantly increases the complexity of bribery problems. For instance, even for election systems whose winner-determination problem is polynomial-time computable, online sequential settings may jump the problem up to high levels of the polynomial hierarchy or even PSPACE-complete. The authors use alternating Turing machines to establish upper bounds and provide examples showing that these bounds are tight for certain election systems.
